sections.summary: Defines scope: applies to commercial sweet varieties of cassava roots (Manihot esculenta Crantz) supplied fresh to the consumer after preparation and packaging, excluding cassava for industrial processing.

Sets overall quality provisions: establishes minimum quality requirements (whole, sound, clean, pest‑free, firm, no off‑odour/taste, etc.), specifies cut dimensions and maturity/transport suitability, and introduces a three‑class classification system (Extra, Class I, Class II) with class‑specific defect allowances.

Lists minimum requirements applicable to all classes: cassava must be whole, sound, clean, practically free of pests and mechanical damage, firm, without abnormal external moisture or loss of flesh colour, with distal cut ≤2 cm and stalk end clean cut 1–2.5 cm, and sufficiently mature and robust to withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition.

Classifies cassava into three classes and notes that 'sweet' varieties contain <50 mg/kg hydrogen cyanide (fresh weight) and must be peeled and fully cooked before consumption; specific class definitions and allowed defects are provided in subsections.

'Extra' class requires cassava of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type and essentially free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or package presentation.

Class I permits cassava of good quality characteristic of the variety with limited slight defects allowed: minor shape defects, scarring/healed damage ≤5% of surface, and scraped areas ≤10%, provided the pulp is unaffected.

Class II covers cassava not qualifying for higher classes but meeting minimum requirements, allowing defects such as shape defects, scarring/healed damage ≤10% of surface and scraped areas ≤20%, provided the pulp is not affected.
Defines sizing: size determined by diameter at thickest cross‑section with Size A 3.5–6.0 cm, B 6.1–8.0 cm, C >8.0 cm, and a minimum weight of 300 g and minimum length of 20 cm.

Specifies tolerances: quality tolerances per class (Extra: up to 5% by number/weight not meeting class but meeting Class I; Class I: up to 10% meeting Class II; Class II: up to 10% not meeting the class or minimum requirements except produce unfit for consumption) and size tolerances of 10% by number/weight allowed to be the size immediately above and/or below that marked on the package.

Sets quality and size tolerances for cassava classes: 'Extra' allows up to 5% by number or weight not meeting class requirements but meeting Class I (or exceptionally within Class I tolerances), Class I allows up to 10% not meeting its requirements but meeting Class II (or exceptionally within Class II tolerances), Class II allows up to 10% that meet neither the class nor minimum requirements excluding produce rendered unfit by rotting or severe deterioration; for all classes, 10% by number or weight may deviate to the next size above or below the package-indicated size.
For 'Extra' class cassava, up to 5% by number or weight may fail the class requirements provided those items meet Class I requirements or, exceptionally, fall within Class I tolerances.
For Class I cassava, up to 10% by number or weight may fail the class requirements provided those items meet Class II requirements or, exceptionally, fall within Class II tolerances.

For Class II cassava, up to 10% by number or weight may fail both the class and minimum requirements, except that produce affected by rotting or other deterioration rendering it unfit for consumption is excluded.
Size tolerances for all cassava classes permit 10% by number or weight of produce to correspond to the size immediately above and/or below the size indicated on the package.

Presentation rules require package contents to be uniform in shape, origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size with the visible portion representative; packaging must protect cassava using new, clean, food-grade materials and non-toxic inks/glues, comply with CAC/RCP 44-1995, and containers must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance requirements and be free of foreign matter and smell.
Each package of cassava must be uniform in shape and contain only produce of the same origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size, and the visible contents must represent the whole package.

Cassava must be packed to protect the produce using new, clean, food-grade materials (printing/labels with non-toxic ink/glue), packed in compliance with CAC/RCP 44-1995, and containers must provide appropriate quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance and be free of foreign matter and smell.
Containers for cassava must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance characteristics to ensure safe handling, shipping and preservation, and be free of all foreign matter and smell.

Labelling requirements supplement CODEX STAN 1-1985: consumer packages must show produce name/type (and may show variety) and include a statement that cassava must be peeled and fully cooked, while non-retail containers must bear legible identification, nature, origin and commercial particulars (class, size, net weight, preparation instructions) and may display an official inspection mark.

In addition to general prepackaged food labelling, consumer packages of cassava must be labelled with the product name and type (variety optional) and must include a statement that cassava should be peeled and fully cooked before consumption.
Each consumer package must be labelled with the name of the produce and its type (sweet), and may optionally indicate the variety.
Packages must include a required statement that cassava should be peeled and fully cooked before being consumed.
Non-retail containers must bear specified particulars (grouped on the same side) legibly and indelibly visible from the outside or be provided in accompanying shipment documents.

Identification on non-retail containers must show the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher and may include an optional identification code, with a footnote clarifying that recycled material of food-grade quality is acceptable for packaging materials.
Non-retail containers must state the name of the produce and its type (sweet) if the contents are not visible, and may optionally indicate the variety.
Labels must declare the country of origin and may optionally include the district or national/regional/local place name where the cassava was grown.

Commercial identification on non-retail containers must indicate class, size (size code or minimum and maximum diameter in centimetres), net weight, and include preparation instructions as required for consumer packages.
Non-substantive heading noting that an official inspection mark may optionally be applied to the packaging.

Cassava must comply with maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CODEX STAN 193-1995), with importing countries to set an acceptable hydrogen cyanide maximum if Codex has none, and must comply with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Hygiene: recommends that covered fresh produce be prepared and handled per Codex Codes of Practice (General Principles of Food Hygiene, Fresh Fruits and Vegetables, and other relevant texts) and comply with any Codex-established microbiological criteria.
